does: This survey provides a comprehensive technical analysis of reinforcement learning methods used to improve large language models after pretraining and supervised fine-tuning. It unifies diverse post-training approaches—including Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) methods like Direct Preference Optimization and Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards approaches like PPO and GRPO—under a single policy gradient framework. The work derives this unifying framework that treats pretraining, SFT, RLHF, and RLVR as special cases, offering detailed technical comparisons of how these methods address persistent problems in LLMs such as harmful outputs, misalignment, and poor performance in domains like mathematics and coding. The survey organizes both foundational components and recent advancements in RL-based post-training, making the landscape of these methods technically accessible and internally connected.
connects: This work builds on decades of reinforcement learning research adapted for language models, extending from early RLHF frameworks that incorporated human preferences into training. It connects to foundational policy gradient methods in RL and their application to language generation, while also responding to recent innovations like Direct Preference Optimization that simplified preference-based learning and verifiable reward approaches that use environment feedback. The survey synthesizes work addressing a core problem identified in LLM research: the gap between what models learn during pretraining and SFT versus what users actually need, whether that is safe behavior, factual accuracy, or specialized reasoning. It builds on prior comparative analyses in deep reinforcement learning while extending those frameworks specifically to the challenges of aligning and improving large language models.
enables: By unifying diverse post-training methods under a common mathematical framework, this survey enables researchers to better understand trade-offs between different approaches and identify principled directions for future improvement. It provides tools for designing hybrid or novel post-training methods that leverage insights from both preference-based and reward-verification approaches. The work facilitates the development of more efficient RL-based post-training procedures and clearer criteria for choosing methods based on available supervision signals and computational constraints. It also opens paths toward better integrating multiple reward signals or verification mechanisms, moving toward more robust alignment of LLMs with human intentions. The unified perspective may enable transfer of techniques between the RLHF and RLVR paradigms and support advances in safety-critical domains like code generation and mathematical reasoning.
